figures.caption: Figure 9: Corneal reflections for markers at different orientations. Rotational shifts in the marker are reflected in the glint patterns and used by our PnP framework to estimate 3-DoF orientation. For evaluation, the test board has a 4×4 marker layout to assess performance across different tag sizes. ArUco markers on the same board provide ground-truth pose via a front-facing camera.
Figure 10: Orientation estimationMAE for the 24 cm tag span across distances. Top: head-gaze aligned condition. Bottom: head-gaze offset by 20°. The figure shows calibrated pitch.

Figure 4: Left: Detection accuracy of different sized retroreflective patches over distances of 2 - 5 m. Right: Detection accuracy of different spaced retroreflective patches (16 mm size) over distances of 2 - 4 m.

Figure 3: Experimental setup for characterizing retroreflective marker design parameters. (A) Retroreflective patches of three diameters (12, 16, and 20 mm). (B) Setup for evaluating patch size. (C) Setup for evaluating inter-patch spacing 𝑑 . (D) The marker board is mounted on a robotic arm that sweeps through a range of orientations while participants fixate on the board at different distances.
Figure 12: Corneal reflection with six markers simultaneously in view. GlintMarkers identifies the marker the user is gazing at and decodes its ID.

Figure 11: Calibrated distance estimation accuracy for the 24 cm tag subset. Each point is one frame; colors distinguish participants. The dashed line is 𝑦 = 𝑥 (perfect estimation). MAE = 0.144 m across 2–4 m.

Figure 7: GlintMarkers layout. A N×N grid of retroreflective patches encodes object ID and provides fiducial anchors for PnP pose estimation (3-DoF orientation and distance). Example 3 × 3 and 4 × 4markers are shown.

Figure 13: Object identification accuracy. Per-frame (top) and majority-vote over 5-frame windows (bottom). The “Others” column aggregates predictions matching one of the 26 nontarget library entries.
Figure 8: High-level overview of GlintMarkers’s processing pipeline.
